notes: Works any time of day, like Hindi 'namaste'. Always polite.

english: Are you well? / How are you?
notes: 'sukham' = well-being (same root as Hindi 'sukh'). Add '-aano?' to make it a question.
Super common filler — agree, accept, or just acknowledge.
Used to say something is NOT the case. For 'there isn't', use 'illa'.
Means absence: 'no water' = 'vellam illa'.
Just like Hindi 'chahiye': 'chaaya venam' = 'tea, please / I want tea'.
Polite way to decline. Opposite of 'venam'.
'manass' = mind (like Hindi 'man'). Literally 'it entered the mind'.
Add '-illa' (not) to 'manassilaayi'. A lifesaver for beginners!
Your most useful early phrase. 'pathukke' = slowly.
'enikku' = to me. You'll reuse it constantly.
Reply to 'nandi' (thanks) — like Hindi 'koi baat nahi'.
The 'nj' is one soft sound — like the 'ny' in 'canyon'.
Polite 'you', like Hindi 'aap'. Safe with anyone.
Includes the listener, like Hindi 'hum sab'.
'konjam' = a little. Very useful humble reply!
Same word as Hindi! Kerala runs on it.
Staple food. 'choru venam' = I want rice.
'oru' = one (a/an). '-tharaamo?' = can you give? Polite ordering.
Say this to make any cook happy.

english: Can you make it less spicy?

notes: Put any place in front: 'bus stop evideyaanu?'
Essential for autos and taxis.
'ithu' = this, 'ethra' = how much. The key shopping phrase.
Same as Hindi 'amma'. Most universal word!
Also used to respectfully address any young woman, like Hindi 'didi'.
Also used for any older man, like Hindi 'bhaiya'. Very common.
Same Sanskrit root as Hindi 'kutumb'.
Same Sanskrit root as Hindi 'ratri'.
You'll say this a LOT in Kerala.

hindi: मेरा सिर दर्द कर रहा है

notes: 'dukham' — same Sanskrit root as Hindi 'dukh'.
'ishttam' = liking, from Sanskrit. Fill in food, places, anything.

hindi: मैं बाद में कॉल करता हूँ

notes: 'sahaayam' = help, same Sanskrit root as Hindi 'sahayta'.
'valare' = very, 'nannaayi' = well/good. Universal praise.
Malayalis use English words like 'meeting', 'office', 'work' natively. '...undu' = there is.
Tack 'kazhinjo?' onto anything to ask 'is it finished?'
The '-aam' ending signals willingness — 'I'll take care of it'. Reassuring.
'ippol' = now. Pair a time word with 'venam' to set urgency.
'ayachu' = sent. Past tense rides on the '-u' ending.
Shares a root with Hindi 'sammati'. Use it to agree in a meeting.
Time word + 'venam' sets a deadline. 'naale' = tomorrow.
One of the most-used words in Malayalam: done / over / finished.
'baaki' = remainder, same as Hindi 'baaki'. '...undu' = there is.
'samayam' = time; '-illa' negates it. The all-purpose 'no X'.
'leave' is used as-is in Kerala offices. Ask your boss this.
'veendum' = again, 'nokkaam' = let's look. Defer politely.
'vrithi' = cleanliness, '-aakku' = make. The '-u' makes it a soft request.
'thuni' = cloth/clothes, 'alakkuka' = to wash (clothes).
'paatram' = vessel (Hindi 'paatra'), 'kazhukuka' = to wash.
'vaa' = come (casual); 'varoo' is the softer version.
From 'mathi' (enough). Stop someone politely.
Praise for a job done well. 'nannaayi' = nicely / well.
'AC kedaayi poyi' = the AC broke. Past tense ('poyi' = went).
'pani cheyyuka' = to function; '-unnilla' = isn't (doing).
'sheri/sari' = correct, '-aakku' = make. 'Make it right.'
Reuses 'vellam' (water) + '-illa'. Same trick everywhere.
'current' = electricity in Kerala English. A power cut!
'aakum' = will become/cost — the '-um' future. Ask for a quote.

english: Does this bus go to Ernakulam?
hindi: क्या यह बस एर्णाकुलम जाती है?
notes: 'ee' = this. Add '-o' to 'pokum' (will go) to make a yes/no question.
'ethu' = which. Point and ask.
'tharoo' = please give. The '-oo' makes it polite.
'nirthuka' = to stop. Tell the driver where to halt.
Reuses 'ethra?' (how much). Works for any price.
'adutha' = next. Handy on a long ride.
'vaiki' = got late; '-yo?' makes it a question.
'eppol' = when, 'varum' = will come (the '-um' future).
'aduthathu' = the next one.
For trains. 'ethu' = which.
'vannu' = came/arrived. Past tense via '-u'.
'late' used as-is + 'aanu' (is).
'vicharikkuka' = to think (Hindi 'vichaar'). '-unnu' = present.
'abhipraayam' = opinion — a shared Sanskrit root.
Non-committal. 'aakaam' = it may be / sure.
'sheri/sari' = right + 'aanu' (is). Agree with a point.
'thonnuka' = to feel/reckon; '-unnilla' negates it. Soft disagreement.
'alla' = is not. Disagree without being harsh.
'nallathu' = a good one; 'ithu' = this.
'kooduthal' = more, so 'more good' = better. No special word needed.
Compare likes with 'kooduthal ishtam'.
Everyday gem. 'kuzhappam' = trouble; '-illa' = none.
'mosham' = bad / poor quality.
'pole' = like / similar (Hindi 'jaisa').
'kandu' = saw (past of 'kaanuka'). 'cinema'/'padam' = film.
'padam' = film, 'nannaayirunnu' = was good (past).
'paattu' = song, 'kelkkuka' = to hear. '-unnu' = present.
'namukku ... kaanaam' = let's watch. Reuse the 'let's' pattern.
From 'rasam' (taste/fun). 'rasakaram' = enjoyable.
'bore' used as-is + 'aanu'. You'll hear this a lot.
'ozhivu' = free/vacant + 'samayam' (time).
'vaayikkuka' = to read. '-unnu' = present/ongoing.
'kalikkuka' = to play.
'nadakkuka' = to walk. '-aan pokunnu' = going to (do).
'sangeetham' = music — a shared Sanskrit root.
'namukku ... -aam' = let's. Reuse it with any verb.
Past verbs end in '-i' or '-u'. 'poyi' = went.
'kandu' = saw. Notice the '-u' ending marking the past.
'kazhichu' = ate. Same '-u' past ending.
'vannu' = came. Compare with future 'varum' (will come).
'paranju' = said. Past of 'parayuka'.
'cheythu' = did. A natural way to drill the past.
Add '-um' for the future. 'pokum' = will go.
'kaanum' = will see. The same '-um' ending.
'kazhikkum' = will eat. '-um' = future, for any subject.
'varum' = will come. Contrast with past 'vannu'.
'cheyyum' = will do. The '-um' future ending.
Future question. Watch: 'cheythu' (did) vs 'cheyyum' (will do).
Add '-unnu' for now/ongoing. 'pokunnu' = going.
'kaanunnu' = seeing/watching. '-unnu' = present.
'kazhikkunnu' = eating. Same '-unnu' ending.
'varunnu' = coming. You'll hear this constantly.
'cheyyunnu' = doing. Present '-unnu'.
Present-tense question. One verb, three tenses: cheythu / cheyyum / cheyyunnu.
